blocks:
Ayahs

سَبَّحَ لِلَّهِ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ وَهُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ (١)
Whatever there is in the heavens and the earth glorifies Allah, and He is the All-mighty, the All-wise. (1)
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لِمَ تَقُولُونَ مَا لَا تَفْعَلُونَ (٢)
O you who have faith! Why do you say what you do not do? (2)
كَبُرَ مَقْتًا عِندَ ٱللَّهِ أَن تَقُولُوا۟ مَا لَا تَفْعَلُونَ (٣)
It is greatly outrageous to Allah that you should say what you do not do. (3)
إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُحِبُّ ٱلَّذِينَ يُقَـٰتِلُونَ فِى سَبِيلِهِۦ صَفًّا كَأَنَّهُم بُنْيَـٰنٌ مَّرْصُوصٌ (٤)
Indeed Allah loves those who fight in His way in ranks, as if they were a compact structure. (4)

Content of Surah as-Saff

This Surah essentially revolves around two fundamental axes: first, the superiority of Islam over all celestial religions and the guarantee of its preservation and eternity by Allah; and second, the necessity of Jihad (striving) in the path of protecting and advancing this faith.

In a detailed view, it can be divided into seven sections:

  1. The beginning of the Surah, which starts with the glorification (Tasbih) of Allah, the Almighty, the All-Wise, creating a readiness in hearts to accept the subsequent truths.
  2. An invitation to harmony between speech and action, and avoiding words without deeds.
  3. An invitation to Jihad with firm resolve and complete unity.
  4. A reminder of the breach of covenant by the Children of Israel and the glad tidings of the Messiah (Jesus) regarding the emergence of Islam.
  5. A guarantee of the victory of Islam over all religions.
  6. An effective invitation toward Jihad and a mention of the worldly and otherworldly rewards for the mujahideen (strivers) in the path of Truth.
  7. A brief reference to the lives of the disciples (Hawariyyun) of Christ and drawing inspiration from them.

However, as mentioned, the main axis remains Islam and Jihad.

The name "as-Saff" (The Rank/Row) was chosen for this Surah because of the expression used in the fourth verse. It is also sometimes referred to as Surah "Isa" or Surah "al-Hawariyyun."

It is widely and famously known that this Surah was revealed entirely in Medina. The presence of verses regarding Jihad in this Surah further supports this, as we know that Jihad was never legislated in Mecca.

Virtue of Reciting Surah as-Saff

In a Hadith from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, we read: "Whoever recites Surah Isa (Surah as-Saff), Prophet Jesus (peace be upon him) will send blessings upon him and seek forgiveness for him as long as he is in this world, and on the Day of Resurrection, he will be his companion." [1]

In another Hadith from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام, we read: "Whoever recites Surah as-Saff and continues its recitation in his obligatory and recommended prayers, Allah will place him in the rank of His angels and His commissioned Prophets." 2

[1] and 2: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 9, p. 277; Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 309.

In the Name of Allah, the Most Gracious, the Most Merciful

Whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth glorifies Allah, and He is the Almighty, the All-Wise. (1) O you who have believed, why do you say what you do not do? (2) It is most hateful in the sight of Allah that you say what you do not do. (3) Indeed, Allah loves those who fight in His cause in a row as though they are a [single] structure joined firmly. (4)

Occasion of Revelation

Commentators have mentioned several occasions of revelation for the verse: "Why do you say what you do not do?" which do not differ significantly from one another, including:

  1. There was a group of believers who used to say: "From now on, whenever we face the enemy, we will not turn our backs and we will not flee." However, they did not fulfill their word and fled on the day of Uhud, to the extent that the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله forehead and his blessed tooth were broken.
  2. When Allah described the reward of the martyrs of Badr, a group of companions said: "Now that it is so, we will exert all our strength in future battles." But they fled at Uhud; thus, the above verse was revealed and rebuked them.
  3. A group of believers, before the command for Jihad was revealed, used to say: "If only Allah would show us the best of deeds so that we might perform them." It was not long before Allah informed them that "the best of deeds is sincere faith and Jihad," but this news did not please them, and they hesitated. The verse was revealed and blamed them. [1]

Commentary

Warriors Like a Steel Wall!

The beginning of this Surah also starts with the glorification of Allah, and for this reason, it is counted among the "Musabbihat" Surahs (Surahs that begin with the glorification of God).

It says: "Whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth glorifies Allah" (سَبَّحَ لِلَّهِ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَ ما فِي الْأَرْضِ) [1]. Why should they not glorify Him and deem Him free from every defect and deficiency, while "He is an invincible Power, and a Wise One aware of all things" (وَ هُوَ الْعَزِیزُ الْحَکِیمُ).

As we have said before, this Surah is a Surah of faith, monotheism, and gnosis. Paying attention to the issue of the universal glorification of all beings—which takes place through both their state of being and their expression—and the wondrous system governing them, which is the best evidence for the existence of the Almighty and All-Wise Creator, strengthens the foundations of faith in hearts and paves the way for the command of Jihad.

[1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 9, p. 278; other commentators have also mentioned these occasions of revelation with slight variations. [1] (Footnote): Regarding the nature of the universal glorification of the world's beings, we have spoken many times in this Tafsir, including under verse 44 of Surah al-Isra (Tafsir Nemouneh, Vol. 12, p. 133) and under verse 41 of Surah an-Nur (Vol. 14, p. 497).

Then, as a reproach and rebuke to those who do not act upon their words, He says: "O you who have believed! Why do you say what you do not do?" (یا أَیُّهَا الَّذِینَ آمَنُوا لِمَ تَقُولُونَ ما لا تَفْعَلُونَ) 2. Although according to the occasion of revelation, these verses were revealed regarding discussions of Jihad and the subsequent flight on the day of the Battle of Uhud, we know that occasions of revelation never limit the broad meaning of the verses. Therefore, any kind of speech without action deserves reproach and blame, whether it relates to steadfastness on the field of Jihad or any other positive and constructive action.

Some commentators have considered the addressees in these verses to be outward believers and actual hypocrites, while the address "O you who have believed" and the expressions in the following verses all indicate that the addressees are real believers—but believers who have not yet reached the peak of perfection in faith, and whose words and deeds have not become harmonized.

Then, continuing the same discourse, He adds: "It is most hateful in the sight of Allah that you say what you do not do" (کَبُرَ مَقْتاً عِنْدَ اللَّهِ أَنْ تَقُولُوا ما لا تَفْعَلُونَ) [1]. You sit in friendly gatherings and speak passionately, but when the field of action arrives, everyone flees to a corner. One of the important signs of true believers is that their words and deeds are one hundred percent harmonized, and the further a person moves away from this principle, the further they have moved from the reality of faith.

"Maqt" originally means "intense hatred toward someone who has performed a foul act." Hence, among the pre-Islamic Arabs, when someone married his father's wife, it was called "Nikah al-Maqt" (the marriage of hatred). In the phrase "کَبُرَ مَقْتاً", the word "Maqt" is joined with "Kubura" (to be great), which is also evidence of intensity and magnitude, signifying Allah's great wrath toward speech devoid of action.

Allamah Tabataba'i says in Al-Mizan: "There is a difference between a person saying something they do not intend to do, and not doing something they said they would. The first is evidence of hypocrisy, and the second is evidence of weakness of will." [1] Apparently, the meaning is that sometimes a person says something while intending from the start not to do it—this is a type of hypocrisy. But sometimes they intend to act from the start but later regret it—this is evidence of weakness of will.

In any case, the above verse includes any violation of a covenant, promise, or pledge, and even according to some, it includes vows (Nadhr). Therefore, in the decree to Malik al-Ashtar, we read that Ali عليه السلام said to him: "Beware... of making promises to them and then following your promise with a breach... for breach of promise causes hatred in the sight of Allah and the people. Allah the Almighty said: 'It is most hateful in the sight of Allah that you say what you do not do.'" 2

And in a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read: "The promise of a believer to his brother is a vow that has no expiation (Kaffarah); so whoever breaks it has begun by opposing Allah and has exposed himself to His hatred, and that is His saying: 'O you who have believed, why do you say what you do not do? It is most hateful in the sight of Allah that you say what you do not do.'" [3]

[1]: Some commentators have considered "Kubura" as part of the "verbs of praise and blame" (Tafsir Ruh al-Bayan), and some have understood it as a sense of wonder (Tafsir al-Kashshaf). [1] (Footnote): Al-Mizan, Vol. 19, p. 287. 2: Nahj al-Balagha, Letter 53. [3]: Usul al-Kafi, Vol. 2, Chapter on Breaking Promises.

In the next verse, He brings forward the main issue, which is the issue of Jihad, saying: "Indeed, Allah loves those who fight in His cause in a row as though they are a [single] structure joined firmly" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ یُحِبُّ الَّذِینَ یُقاتِلُونَ فِی سَبِیلِهِ صَفًّا کَأَنَّهُمْ بُنْیانٌ مَرْصُوصٌ) [1]. Therefore, the act of fighting itself is not the only point; what is important is that the fighting be "in the cause of Allah" (فِی سَبِیلِ اللَّهِ), and that too with complete unity and cohesion, like a steel wall.

"Saff" (Row) originally has a gerundial meaning, referring to placing things in a straight line, but here it carries the meaning of an active participle.

"Marsous" is derived from the root "Rasas," meaning lead. Since lead was sometimes melted and poured between the components of buildings to ensure strength and integration, making them exceptionally firm and unified, any solid structure is called "Marsous."

Here, the meaning is that the mujahideen of the path of Truth should stand against the enemy with one heart and one soul, firm and steadfast, as if they are all a single interconnected unit with no gap between them. Thus, we read in the Tafsir of Ali ibn Ibrahim that in explaining this verse, he said: "They line up like a building that never collapses." 2

In a Hadith, it is mentioned that the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, on the field of Siffin, when he wanted to prepare his companions for battle, said: "Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, has guided you to this duty... and said: 'Indeed, Allah loves those who fight in His cause in a row as though they are a [single] structure joined firmly.' Therefore, firm up your ranks like an iron building. Let those clad in armor come forward, and let those without armor stand behind them. Clench your teeth firmly... and weave and dodge before the spears, for it is more effective in parrying the enemy's spear. Do not stare at the mass of the enemy so that your heart may be stronger and your soul more tranquil. Speak less, for it drives away weakness and is more appropriate for your dignity. Do not tilt your banners and do not move them from their places, and do not entrust them except to the hands of the brave!" [1]

Key Points

1. The Necessity of Unity of Ranks One of the most important factors for victory against enemies is the cohesion and interconnectedness of ranks on the battlefield. Not only in military battles but also in political and economic struggles, nothing can be achieved except through unity.

In reality, the Quran likens enemies to a destructive flood that can only be contained by a steel dam. The expression "BUNYANUN MARSOUS" (a solid structure) is the most beautiful expression that exists in this regard. In a great building or dam, each component has a role, but this role becomes effective only if there is no distance or gap between them, and they become so united as if they are nothing but a single unit. They all turn into a single hand and a single great, firm fist that crushes and shatters the enemy's head!

Alas, this great teaching of Islam is forgotten today, and the great Islamic society not only lacks the form of a "solid structure" but has turned into scattered ranks standing against one another, each with a different desire in their head and a whim in their heart.

It must be noted that unity of ranks is not achieved through words and slogans; it requires "unity of purpose" and "unity of belief," and this is something that is impossible without sincerity of intention, real gnosis, correct Islamic upbringing, and the revival of Quranic culture.

If Allah loves the mujahideen who are like a solid structure, then He hates these scattered groups. And right now, we see the signs of Allah's wrath and divine anger in this society of hundreds of millions with our own eyes—one example of which is the dominance of a small group of "Zionists" over Islamic lands. O Allah! Grant us awareness, awakening, and familiarity with the Quran and its life-giving teachings.

2. Speech Without Action The tongue is the translator of the heart, and if the paths of these two separate from each other, it is a sign of hypocrisy. We know that a hypocritical person does not possess health of thought and soul.

One of the worst calamities that can dominate a society is the calamity of the loss of trust, and its main cause is the separation of speech from action. People who speak but do not act can never trust one another or be coordinated in the face of problems. Brotherhood and sincerity will never govern them, they will never have value or worth, and no enemy will take them into account.

When the looters of the Syrian army raided the borders of Iraq and the news reached the ears of Ali عليه السلام, he became deeply distressed. He delivered a sermon and said: "O people whose bodies are together but whose desires are scattered! Your hot words break hard stones, but your weak actions make the enemies crave you. In gatherings and circles, you say such and such, but when the battle comes, you cry out: 'O war, stay away from us!'" [1]

In a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read that he said: "The scholar is the one whose action confirms his speech, and whoever's action does not confirm his speech is not a scholar." 2

One of the contemporary poets has visualized the fate of nations that are people of words and not people of action—and for this reason are always captive in the claws of enemies—in a beautiful story told through the tongues of a nightingale and a hunting hawk:

Last night a nightingale said to a hawk, "Why is your state better than mine? You who are ugly, grim, and fearsome, You who are mute, tongue-tied, and silent. Drunk and free upon the hands of kings, With a hundred graces, you make your home. While I, with such eloquence and sweet song, With a beautiful form and a delicate body— A cage is my home and my day is like night, My portion is grief, sorrow, and toil!" The hawk replied, "You speak the truth, But the secret of it is very clear: Your habit is to speak and not to do, My nature is to do and not to speak!"

[1] (Footnote): "Saffan" here is in the accusative case as a 'Hal' (circumstantial adverb). 2 (Footnote): Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 311. [1] (Footnote): Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 301; a similar meaning also appears in Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 124. [1] (Footnote): Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 29. 2 (Footnote): Usul al-Kafi, Vol. 1, Chapter on the Attribute of Scholars, Hadith 2.

Ayahs

وَإِذْ قَالَ مُوسَىٰ لِقَوْمِهِۦ يَـٰقَوْمِ لِمَ تُؤْذُونَنِى وَقَد تَّعْلَمُونَ أَنِّى رَسُولُ ٱللَّهِ إِلَيْكُمْ ۖ فَلَمَّا زَاغُوٓا۟ أَزَاغَ ٱللَّهُ قُلُوبَهُمْ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ لَا يَهْدِى ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلْفَـٰسِقِينَ (٥)
When Moses said to his people, ‘O my people! Why do you torment me, when you certainly know that I am Allah’s apostle to you?’ So when they swerved [from the right path], Allah made their hearts swerve, and Allah does not guide the transgressing lot. (5)
وَإِذْ قَالَ عِيسَى ٱبْنُ مَرْيَمَ يَـٰبَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ إِنِّى رَسُولُ ٱللَّهِ إِلَيْكُم مُّصَدِّقًا لِّمَا بَيْنَ يَدَىَّ مِنَ ٱلتَّوْرَىٰةِ وَمُبَشِّرًۢا بِرَسُولٍ يَأْتِى مِنۢ بَعْدِى ٱسْمُهُۥٓ أَحْمَدُ ۖ فَلَمَّا جَآءَهُم بِٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ قَالُوا۟ هَـٰذَا سِحْرٌ مُّبِينٌ (٦)
And when Jesus son of Mary said, ‘O Children of Israel! Indeed I am the apostle of Allah to you, to confirm what is before me of the Torah, and to give the good news of an apostle who will come after me, whose name is Ahmad.’ But when he brought them manifest proofs, they said, ‘This is plain magic.’ (6)

Commentary

I Have Brought the Glad Tidings of the Advent of Ahmad!

Following the two commands mentioned in the previous verses regarding the "harmony of speech and action" and "unity of ranks," these verses point to a portion of the lives of two Prophets, Moses (عليه السلام) and Messiah (عليه السلام), to complete this meaning. Unfortunately, clear examples of the "separation of speech and action" and "lack of cohesion in ranks" were seen in the lives of their followers, along with the grim fate that followed.

First, He says: "And remember when Moses said to his people: 'O my people! Why do you hurt me while you certainly know that I am the messenger of Allah to you?'" (وَ إِذْ قالَ مُوسى‌ لِقَوْمِهِ يا قَوْمِ لِمَ تُؤْذُونَنِي وَ قَدْ تَعْلَمُونَ أَنِّي رَسُولُ اللَّهِ إِلَيْكُمْ‌).

This "hurting" may refer to all the oppositions and excuse-making that the Children of Israel displayed toward this great Prophet throughout the life of Moses (عليه السلام). Or, it may refer to an incident like that of "Bayt al-Maqdis," where they told Moses: "We are not willing to enter this city and fight the 'Amalika' (giants) who are a powerful and tyrannical group; you and your Lord go and conquer it so that we may enter!" (Al-Ma'idah: 24).

This very matter caused them to remain in the wilderness of "Tih" (the Sinai wandering) for many years, tasting the bitter flavor of vain claims and lethargy in the matter of Jihad.

However, considering what is mentioned in verse 69 of Surah Al-Ahzab, it seems that this "hurting" refers to the unjust attributions they made against Moses, from which Allah cleared him. In that verse, we read: "O you who have believed, be not like those who abused Moses; then Allah cleared him of what they said. And he, in the sight of Allah, was distinguished" (يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا تَكُونُوا كَالَّذِينَ آذَوْا مُوسى‌ فَبَرَّأَهُ اللَّهُ مِمَّا قالُوا وَ كانَ عِنْدَ اللَّهِ وَجِيهاً).

These attributions were many: sometimes they accused him of murdering his brother Aaron (Harun)! Sometimes (God forbid) they accused him of illicit relations with a prostitute (a conspiracy hatched by the deceitful Korah/Qarun so he would not have to submit to the command of Zakat!). At other times, they accused him of sorcery, madness, or certain physical defects, the details of which are mentioned under the aforementioned verse in Surah Al-Ahzab [1]. How is it possible for someone to claim faith in a Prophet and yet make such attributions to him? Is this not the clearest example of the separation of speech from action? Therefore, Moses says: "While you are certain that I am the Messenger of Allah, what are these improper words?!" But this action did not go unpunished, as we read at the end of the verse under discussion: "So when they deviated, Allah caused their hearts to deviate. And Allah does not guide the defiantly disobedient people" (فَلَمَّا زاغُوا أَزاغَ اللَّهُ قُلُوبَهُمْ وَ اللَّهُ لا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الْفاسِقِينَ‌).

What calamity is greater than this? That a human being is deprived of Divine guidance and his heart deviates from the truth [2]?

From this expression, it is understood that although guidance and misguidance are from Allah's side, their grounds, preliminaries, and factors are from the human being himself. Because on one hand, He says: "When they deviated from the truth, Allah caused their hearts to deviate," meaning they took the first step. On the other hand, He says: "Allah does not guide the transgressing (fasiq) people." First, a transgression or sin occurs, making the person deserve the withdrawal of Divine success (tawfiq) and guidance, and then they are afflicted with this great deprivation.

A detailed discussion on this subject is provided under verse 36 of Surah Az-Zumar (refer to Volume 19 of Tafsir Nemouneh, pages 461 to 468).

[1]: Tafsir Nemouneh, Vol. 17, pp. 444-446. [2]: "Zāghū" is from the root "zaygh," meaning deviation from the straight path.

In the next verse, it refers to the mission of Prophet Jesus (عليه السلام) and the obstruction and denial of the Children of Israel against him, adding: "And remember when Jesus, the son of Mary, said: 'O Children of Israel! Indeed I am the messenger of Allah to you, confirming what came before me of the Torah'" (وَ إِذْ قالَ عِيسَى ابْنُ مَرْيَمَ يا بَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ إِنِّي رَسُولُ اللَّهِ إِلَيْكُمْ مُصَدِّقاً لِما بَيْنَ يَدَيَّ مِنَ التَّوْراةِ). "And bringing glad tidings of a messenger who will come after me, whose name is Ahmad" (وَ مُبَشِّراً بِرَسُولٍ يَأْتِي مِنْ بَعْدِي اسْمُهُ أَحْمَدُ).

Therefore, I am the connecting link that joins the nation of Moses and his book to the nation of the future Prophet (the Prophet of Islam) and his book.

In this way, the Messiah (عليه السلام) had no claim other than Divine messengership, and that too for a specific period of time; whatever they have attributed to him regarding divinity or being the son of God is all falsehood and lies.

Although a group of the Children of Israel believed in this promised Prophet, a vast group stood stubbornly against him and even denied his clear miracles. Thus, at the end of the verse, it adds: "But when he (Ahmad) came to them with clear evidences, they said: 'This is obvious magic!'" (فَلَمَّا جاءَهُمْ بِالْبَيِّناتِ قالُوا هذا سِحْرٌ مُبِينٌ‌).

It is strange that the Jewish tribe had recognized this Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) even before the Arab polytheists, and out of their longing to meet him, they left their homelands and settled in the land of Medina. Yet, despite all this, many idol-worshippers eventually recognized and believed in this promised Prophet, while many Jews remained in their stubbornness, enmity, and denial.

A group of commentators has returned the pronoun in "فَلَمَّا جاءَهُمْ" (when he came to them) to the Prophet of Islam (Ahmad), as we mentioned above. However, some believe the pronoun refers to Jesus (عليه السلام), meaning when Jesus (عليه السلام) brought clear miracles to the Children of Israel, they denied them and called them magic.

But the subsequent verses show that the first interpretation is more correct, as the emphasis in these verses is on Islam and the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله).

Key Points

1. The Relationship Between Glad Tidings and the Perfection of Religion

The expression "glad tidings" (basharat) regarding Jesus' (عليه السلام) announcement of the advent of Islam is a subtle reference to the perfection of this religion compared to previous religions. A study of Quranic verses and a comparison of Islamic knowledge and teachings in the fields of beliefs, rulings, laws, and moral and social issues with what is found in the books of the Two Testaments (Torah and Gospel) clearly demonstrates this superiority.

Although the above verse does not explicitly mention whether this glad tiding was in the text of the Messiah's heavenly book or not, other verses of the Quran testify to its mention in the Gospel itself. In verse 156 of Al-A'raf, we read: "Those who follow the Messenger, the unlettered prophet, whom they find written [i.e., described] with them in the Torah and the Gospel..." (الَّذِينَ يَتَّبِعُونَ الرَّسُولَ النَّبِيَّ الْأُمِّيَّ الَّذِي يَجِدُونَهُ مَكْتُوباً عِنْدَهُمْ فِي التَّوْراةِ وَ الْإِنْجِيلِ), and some other verses [1].

2. Glad Tidings in the Two Testaments and the Term "Paraclete"

Undoubtedly, what is in the hands of the Jews and Christians today as the Torah and Gospel are not the books revealed to the great Prophets of God, namely Moses and the Messiah. Rather, they are a collection of books authored by their companions or individuals who came into existence after them. A brief study of these books is living proof of this claim; the Christians and Jews themselves make no claim otherwise.

Nevertheless, there is no doubt that a portion of the teachings of Moses and Jesus and the content of their heavenly books were transferred to these books through the sayings of their followers. For this reason, one cannot accept everything in the Old Testament (the Torah and related books) and the New Testament (the Gospel and related books), nor is all of it deniable. Rather, it is a mixture of the teachings of these two great Prophets with the thoughts and ideas of others.

In any case, in the existing books, many expressions are seen that give glad tidings of the appearance of a great figure whose signs apply to none but Islam and its bringer.

It is noteworthy that, besides the prophecies seen in these books that apply to the person of the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله), the word "Paraclete" (Faraqlita) is emphasized in three places in the Gospel of John [1], which has been translated as "Comforter" in Persian translations. Now, pay attention to the text of the Gospel of John:

"And I will pray the Father, and He shall give you another 'Comforter,' that he may abide with you forever" [2]. In the next chapter, it says: "But when the 'Comforter' is come, whom I will send unto you from the Father, even the Spirit of truth, which proceedeth from the Father, he shall testify of me" [3]. And in the following chapter, we read: "Nevertheless I tell you the truth; It is expedient for you that I go away: for if I go not away, the 'Comforter' will not come unto you; but if I depart, I will send him unto you" [4]!

The important point is that in the Syriac text of the Gospels, which is taken from the Greek original, instead of "Comforter," the word "Paraqlita" appears, and in the Greek text, it is "Periclytos," which in Greek culture means "the praised one," equivalent to "Muhammad" or "Ahmad."

However, when the church authorities saw that the publication of such a translation would deal a heavy blow to their establishment, they wrote "Paracletos" instead of "Periclytos"! This means "Comforter," and with this obvious distortion, they altered this living document. Even so, despite this distortion, it remains a clear glad tiding of a great future advent [5].

[1]: This expression appears in the Arabic Gospel printed in London, William Watts Press, 1857. [2]: Gospel of John, Chapter 14, Verse 16. [3]: Gospel of John, Chapter 15, Verse 26. [4]: Gospel of John, Chapter 16, Verse 7. [5]: Al-Furqan fi Tafsir al-Quran, Vol. 27 & 28, p. 306, under the verse discussed—where the Syriac text of the above sentences is also precisely provided.

In the first volume of Tafsir Nemouneh, we quoted the eloquent testimony of a famous Christian priest who later embraced Islam (Fakhr al-Islam, author of the famous book Anis al-A'lam), citing his great teacher regarding the interpretation of "Paraclete," which clarifies that these glad tidings were about a person named "Ahmad" and "Muhammad" (صلى الله عليه وآله) [1]. Here, we draw your attention to the translation of a passage from the Great French Encyclopedia on this subject:

"Muhammad is the founder of the religion of Islam, the Messenger of God, and the Seal of the Prophets. The word 'Muhammad' means 'the much-praised' and is derived from the root 'Hamd,' which means glorification and praise. By a strange coincidence, another name, which is also from the root 'Hamd' and is synonymous with the word Muhammad—namely 'Ahmad'—is mentioned, which it is highly probable the Christians of Arabia used instead of 'Paraclete.' Ahmad, meaning the highly praised and very illustrious, is the translation of the word 'Periclytos,' for which the word 'Paracletos' was mistakenly substituted. Thus, Muslim religious writers have repeatedly pointed out that the intent of this word is the glad tiding of the advent of the Prophet of Islam. The Holy Quran also clearly refers to this subject in the amazing verse of Surah As-Saff" [2]. In short, the meaning of "Paraclete" is not the Holy Spirit or the Comforter, but rather a concept equivalent to "Ahmad" (take note).

[1]: Tafsir Nemouneh, Vol. 1, Surah Al-Baqarah, under Verse 41. [2]: Great French Encyclopedia, Vol. 23, p. 4176.

3. Is the Name of the Prophet of Islam "Ahmad"?

An important question raised here is that the well-known name of the Prophet of Islam is "Muhammad," while in the verse under discussion, he is mentioned as "Ahmad." How are these two consistent?

In response to this question, it is necessary to note the following points:

A) It is recorded in histories that the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) had two names from childhood, and people even addressed him by both names: one "Muhammad" and the other "Ahmad." The first was chosen for him by his grandfather, Abdul Muttalib, and the second by his mother, Aminah. This matter is mentioned in detail in Sirah al-Halabiyyah.

B) Among those who repeatedly referred to the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) by this name was his uncle, Abu Talib. Even today, in the book known as Diwan Abu Talib in our hands, many poems are seen in which the noble Prophet of Islam is referred to as "Ahmad," such as: "The oppressors intended to kill Ahmad * But they found no leader among them for his killing." "And if Ahmad has come to them * With truth, and has not brought them falsehood" [1]. Outside of Diwan Abu Talib, poems from Abu Talib have also been narrated in this regard, such as: "Allah has honored the Prophet Muhammad * Thus the most honorable of Allah's creation among people is Ahmad" [2].

[1]: Diwan Abu Talib, pp. 25-29. [2]: Tarikh Ibn Asakir, Vol. 1, p. 275.

C) In the poems of "Hassan ibn Thabit," the famous poet of the Prophet's era, this expression is also seen: "A grief-stricken one whom the loss of Ahmad had made thin * And she continued to enumerate the gifts and boons of the Messenger of Allah" [1]. The poems from Abu Talib or others in which the name Ahmad (instead of Muhammad) appears are so numerous that there is no room to quote them all here. We end this discussion with another interesting poem from the son of Abu Talib, namely Ali (عليه السلام): "Do you command me to be patient in aiding 'Ahmad'? * By Allah, I did not say what I said out of distress." "I shall strive for the sake of Allah in aiding 'Ahmad' * The Prophet of guidance, who was praised (Mahmud) as a child and as a youth" [2].

D) In the narrations regarding the event of the Mi'raj (Ascension), we frequently read that Allah addressed the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) many times on the night of Mi'raj as "Ahmad." Perhaps it is from here that it became famous that his name in the heavens is "Ahmad" and on earth is "Muhammad" (صلى الله عليه وآله). In a hadith from Imam al-Baqir (عليه السلام), it is also mentioned that the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) had ten names, five of which are in the Quran: "Muhammad," "Ahmad," "Abdullah," "Ya-Sin," and "Nun" [3].

[1]: Diwan Hassan ibn Thabit, p. 59, edited by Muhammad Izzat Nasrullah. [2]: Al-Ghadir, Vol. 7, p. 358. [3]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 313—In Tafsir al-Durr al-Manthur, narrations are also mentioned in this regard, the full citation of which would be lengthy. Vol. 6, p. 214.

E) When the Prophet recited the above verses (the verses of Surah As-Saff) to the people of Medina and Mecca, and they certainly reached the ears of the People of the Book, none of the polytheists or the People of the Book objected that "the Gospel gave glad tidings of the coming of 'Ahmad,' while your name is 'Muhammad'." This silence itself is proof of the fame of this name in that environment, for if an objection had been made, it would have been narrated to us, as the objections of the enemies, even in cases where they were very offensive, are recorded in history.

From the sum of this discussion, we conclude that the name Ahmad was among the well-known names of the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) [1].

[1]: In this discussion and the previous one, the books Ahmad-e Mow'ud-e Injil and Tafsir Furqan were also utilized.

Ayahs

وَمَنْ أَظْلَمُ مِمَّنِ ٱفْتَرَىٰ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ ٱلْكَذِبَ وَهُوَ يُدْعَىٰٓ إِلَى ٱلْإِسْلَـٰمِ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ لَا يَهْدِى ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (٧)
Who is a greater wrongdoer than him who fabricates falsehoods against Allah, while he is being summoned to Islam? And Allah does not guide the wrongdoing lot. (7)
يُرِيدُونَ لِيُطْفِـُٔوا۟ نُورَ ٱللَّهِ بِأَفْوَٰهِهِمْ وَٱللَّهُ مُتِمُّ نُورِهِۦ وَلَوْ كَرِهَ ٱلْكَـٰفِرُونَ (٨)
They desire to put out the light of Allah with their mouths, but Allah will perfect His light though the faithless should be averse. (8)
هُوَ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَرْسَلَ رَسُولَهُۥ بِٱلْهُدَىٰ وَدِينِ ٱلْحَقِّ لِيُظْهِرَهُۥ عَلَى ٱلدِّينِ كُلِّهِۦ وَلَوْ كَرِهَ ٱلْمُشْرِكُونَ (٩)
It is He who has sent His Apostle with guidance and the true religion that He may make it prevail over all religions though the polytheists should be averse. (9)

Commentary

They Want to Extinguish Allah’s Light with Their Mouths?!

In the preceding verses, we read how a group of stubborn antagonists rose in opposition and denial, despite the glad tidings of the previous Prophet, Christ عليه السلام, regarding the emergence of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, and despite the fact that the call of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله was accompanied by "clear proofs" (bayyinat), evident arguments, and miracles. The verses under discussion describe the end of these individuals' efforts and their ultimate fate.

First, He says: "And who is more unjust than he who invents a lie against Allah while he is being invited to Islam?" (وَ مَنْ أَظْلَمُ مِمَّنِ افْتَرى‌ عَلَى اللَّهِ الْكَذِبَ وَ هُوَ يُدْعى‌ إِلَى الْإِسْلامِ‌).

Indeed, such a person—who considers the call of the Divine Prophet a lie, his miracle as sorcery, and his religion as falsehood—is the most unjust of people. This is because he closes the path of guidance and salvation for himself and other servants of Allah, depriving them of this source of Divine grace which is the guarantor of their eternal felicity.

At the end of the verse, He adds: "And Allah does not guide the wrongdoing people" (وَ اللَّهُ لا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الظَّالِمِينَ‌).

Allah’s work is guidance, and His Pure Essence is spiritual light and radiance: "Allah is the Light of the heavens and the earth." However, guidance requires a receptive ground, and this ground does not exist in those who are enemies of the truth.

This verse is once again an emphasis on the reality that although guidance and misguidance are from Allah, their preliminaries begin from the human being himself; for this reason, fatalism (jabr) never follows.

The phrase "while he is being invited to Islam" (وَ هُوَ يُدْعى‌ إِلَى الْإِسْلامِ‌) points to the fact that the Prophet’s صلى الله عليه وآله invitation encompasses the well-being of this world and the Hereafter and the salvation of humanity. Given this, how can a person strike at the very root of his own happiness?

The expression "Who is more unjust..." (مَنْ أَظْلَمُ) is repeated fifteen times in the Holy Quran, and the verse under discussion is the last of them. Although their contexts appear to be different, this might raise the question: can there be more than one group who are the "most unjust of people"? However, a careful look at these verses shows that they all return to the denial of Divine signs and preventing people from the path of truth. In reality, there is no greater injustice than preventing people from reaching such a program which is the path to prosperity in all fields.

Tafsir Nemouneh, Vol. 24, p. 82

  • Then, to show that the enemies of truth are unable to dismantle His religion, He states through a beautiful metaphor: "They intend to extinguish the light of Allah with their mouths, but Allah will perfect His light, although the disbelievers dislike it" (يُرِيدُونَ لِيُطْفِؤُا نُورَ اللَّهِ بِأَفْواهِهِمْ وَ اللَّهُ مُتِمُّ نُورِهِ وَ لَوْ كَرِهَ الْكافِرُونَ‌).

They are like someone who wants to extinguish the light of the world-illuminating sun by blowing at it. They are bats who imagine that if they close their eyes to the sun and immerse themselves in the veils of the night's darkness, they can rise to oppose this fountain of light.

The history of Islam is a living document of the objective realization of this great Quranic prophecy, for from the first day of Islam's emergence, various conspiracies were hatched for its destruction:

Sometimes through the mockery and harassment of enemies. Sometimes through economic and social blockades. Sometimes through the imposition of various wars on the battlefields of Uhud, Ahzab, Hunayn, and others. Sometimes through the internal conspiracies of the hypocrites. Sometimes by creating division among the ranks of the Muslims. Sometimes through the Crusades. Sometimes through the occupation of the land of Quds and the first Qibla of the Muslims. Sometimes through the division of the great Islamic nation into more than forty countries! Sometimes through programs to change the script and sever the Muslim youth from their ancient culture. Sometimes through the promotion of indecency and means of moral corruption and ideological deviation among the youth. Sometimes through military, political, and economic colonialism. And sometimes through other means...

But just as Allah had willed, this Divine light is expanding day by day, and the reach of Islam becomes wider than in the past at every moment. Statistics show that the population of Muslims in the world is increasing despite the joint efforts of "Zionists," "Crusaders," and "Eastern Materialists." Yes, they constantly want to extinguish Allah’s light, but Allah has another will.

And this is an eternal miracle of the Quran.

It is noteworthy that this theme is mentioned twice in the verses of the Holy Quran, with the difference that in one instance it says "they intend that they extinguish" (يُرِيدُونَ أَنْ يُطْفِؤُا) (At-Tawbah: 32), but here it is "they intend to extinguish" (يُرِيدُونَ لِيُطْفِؤُا).

Raghib in Al-Mufradat, explaining this difference, says: The first verse refers to extinguishing without preliminaries, while the second refers to extinguishing by resorting to preliminaries. That is, whether they make preparations or not, they are unable to extinguish the Divine light!

In the last verse under discussion, for further emphasis, He says explicitly: "He is the One who has sent His Messenger with guidance and the religion of truth to manifest it over all religions, although the polytheists dislike it" (هُوَ الَّذِي أَرْسَلَ رَسُولَهُ بِالْهُدى‌ وَ دِينِ الْحَقِّ لِيُظْهِرَهُ عَلَى الدِّينِ كُلِّهِ وَ لَوْ كَرِهَ الْمُشْرِكُونَ‌).

The expression "sent His Messenger with guidance and the religion of truth" serves as an explanation of the secret behind Islam's victory and dominance, because this victory is inherent in the nature of "guidance" and the "religion of truth." Islam and the Quran are Divine light, and light shows its effects wherever it is and is the cause of victory; the dislike of the polytheists and disbelievers cannot create a barrier in this path.

Interestingly, this verse also appears three times in the Holy Quran with slight variations: Once in Surah At-Tawbah (Verse 33), once in Surah Al-Fath (Verse 28), and once in this Surah As-Saff.

However, it should not be forgotten that this repetition and emphasis occurred at a time when Islam had not yet established itself in the Arabian Peninsula, let alone other parts of the world. Yet the Quran emphatically relied on this matter at that very time, and future events proved the truth of this great prophecy. Ultimately, Islam prevailed over other religions both in terms of logic and practical progress, pushing back enemies from vast parts of the world and taking their place, and it is still advancing today.

Of course, the final stage of this advancement, in our belief, will be realized with the appearance of Hazrat Mahdi ارواحنا فداه (may our souls be sacrificed for him), and these verses are themselves a proof of that great appearance.

Regarding the content of this verse—whether the intended dominance is logical dominance or dominance of power—and how it relates to the appearance of Hazrat Mahdi عليه السلام, we have provided more detailed discussions under Verse 33 of Surah At-Tawbah. [^1]

[^1]: Refer to Tafsir Nemouneh, Volume 7, pages 368 to 389.

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ هَلْ أَدُلُّكُمْ عَلَىٰ تِجَـٰرَةٍ تُنجِيكُم مِّنْ عَذَابٍ أَلِيمٍ (١٠)
O you who have faith! Shall I show you a deal that will deliver you from a painful punishment? (10)
تُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِۦ وَتُجَـٰهِدُونَ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ بِأَمْوَٰلِكُمْ وَأَنفُسِكُمْ ۚ ذَٰلِكُمْ خَيْرٌ لَّكُمْ إِن كُنتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ (١١)
Have faith in Allah and His Apostle, and wage jihad in the way of Allah with your persons and possessions. That is better for you, should you know. (11)
يَغْفِرْ لَكُمْ ذُنُوبَكُمْ وَيُدْخِلْكُمْ جَنَّـٰتٍ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ وَمَسَـٰكِنَ طَيِّبَةً فِى جَنَّـٰتِ عَدْنٍ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ ٱلْفَوْزُ ٱلْعَظِيمُ (١٢)
He will forgive your sins and admit you into gardens with streams running in them, and into good dwellings in the Gardens of Eden; that is the great success. (12)
وَأُخْرَىٰ تُحِبُّونَهَا ۖ نَصْرٌ مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ وَفَتْحٌ قَرِيبٌ ۗ وَبَشِّرِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (١٣)
And other [blessings] you cherish: help from Allah and a victory near at hand, and give good news to the faithful. (13)

Commentary

A Highly Profitable and Peerless Trade!

As we stated at the beginning of the Surah, one of the primary objectives of this chapter is the invitation to faith and Jihad. The verses under discussion serve as an emphasis on these two principles, using a refined metaphor that creates a divine motivation within the human soul—a motivation that is the prerequisite for the victory of Islam over all religions, as alluded to in the preceding verses.

First, He says: "O you who have believed! Shall I guide you to a transaction that will save you from a painful punishment?" (يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا هَلْ أَدُلُّكُمْ عَلَىٰ تِجَارَةٍ تُنْجِيكُمْ مِنْ عَذَابٍ أَلِيمٍ).

Even though "faith" and "Jihad" are definitive obligations, here He presents them not in the form of a command, but as a proposal for a trade, adorned with expressions that speak of Allah's infinite grace.

Undoubtedly, "salvation from a painful punishment" is among the most important desires of every human being. Therefore, the question—"Do you want Me to guide you to a trade that will deliver you from a painful punishment?"—is captivating to everyone. Once He has drawn hearts toward Himself with this question, without waiting for their response, He proceeds to describe this profitable trade, adding: "It is that...

...you believe in Allah and His Messenger and strive in the cause of Allah with your wealth and your lives" (تُؤْمِنُونَ بِاللَّهِ وَ رَسُولِهِ وَ تُجاهِدُونَ فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ بِأَمْوالِكُمْ وَ أَنْفُسِكُمْ) [1]. Certainly, Allah has no need for this profitable trade; rather, all its benefits belong entirely to the believers. Thus, at the end of the verse, He says: "That is best for you, if you only knew" (ذلِكُمْ خَيْرٌ لَكُمْ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ).

It is noteworthy that the addressees are the believers, as evidenced by the phrase "O you who have believed," yet He still invites them to the two principles of "faith" and "Jihad." This expression may suggest that nominal and superficial faith is not enough; a deep and pure faith is required—one that can become the source of sacrifice, devotion, and Jihad. It is also possible that the mention of faith in Allah and the Messenger here is an elaboration of the "faith" mentioned briefly at the start of the previous verse.

In any case, faith in the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله is inseparable from faith in Allah, just as Jihad with one's life cannot be separated from Jihad with one's wealth. This is because all battles require means and resources that must be provided through financial contributions. Some are capable of both types of Jihad, some are only capable of Jihad with wealth and supporting the home front, and sometimes they possess only their lives and are ready to offer them.

However, in any case, these two types of Jihad must be coupled together to achieve victory. If we see that Jihad with wealth is mentioned first, it is not because it is more important than Jihad with one's life, but because it is considered a prerequisite for it, as the tools of Jihad are provided through financial assistance.

[1]: The sentence "تُؤْمِنُونَ بِاللَّهِ..." is an "explanatory sentence" (istinafiyyah) that interprets the trade, and some have considered it an "appositive" (atf al-bayan). In any case, this "declarative sentence" carries the meaning of a "command."

Up to this point, three essential pillars of this great and peerless trade have been identified: the "Buyer" is Allah, the "Sellers" are the people of faith, and the "Merchandise" is their lives and wealth. Now comes the fourth pillar, which is the price of this magnificent transaction.

He says: "If you do so, He will forgive you your sins and admit you into gardens beneath which rivers flow and pleasant dwellings in gardens of perpetual residence. That is the great attainment" (يَغْفِرْ لَكُمْ ذُنُوبَكُمْ وَ يُدْخِلْكُمْ جَنَّاتٍ تَجْرِي مِنْ تَحْتِهَا الْأَنْهارُ وَ مَساكِنَ طَيِّبَةً فِي جَنَّاتِ عَدْنٍ ذلِكَ الْفَوْزُ الْعَظِيمُ) [1]. In the stage of otherworldly reward, He first addresses the forgiveness of sins, because the greatest mental distress for a human stems from his sins. Once forgiveness and pardon are certain, there is no sorrow. This expression shows that the first divine gift to the martyrs in His cause is that He forgives all their sins.

But does this only refer to the "Rights of Allah" (Haqq-Allah), or does it also include the "Rights of People" (Haqq-an-Nas)? The absolute nature of the verse suggests generality, but given that Allah has left the Rights of People to the people themselves, some have doubted the verse's generality in that regard.

Thus, in the above verses, two branches of faith (faith in Allah and the Messenger), two branches of Jihad (Jihad with wealth and life), and two branches of otherworldly rewards...

[1]: The sentence "يَغْفِرْ لَكُمْ..." serves as the "result" (jazaa') for an omitted "condition" (shart) inferred from the previous verse, as if it were: "If you believe in Allah and His Messenger and strive in His cause... He will forgive you your sins..." It is also possible that it is the response to the "command" inferred from the declarative sentences "you believe" and "you strive."

...(forgiveness of sins and entry into eternal Paradise) have been mentioned. And as we shall see, two branches of divine favors in this world are also mentioned in the following verse.

Where He says: "And another [favor] that you love and cherish He will grant you: help from Allah and a near victory" (وَ أُخْرى‌ تُحِبُّونَها نَصْرٌ مِنَ اللَّهِ وَ فَتْحٌ قَرِيبٌ) [1]. What a profitable and blessed trade! One that is entirely victory, triumph, blessing, and mercy; for this reason, it is referred to as the "great attainment" and a massive victory. Consequently, He congratulates the believers on this great trade and gives them glad tidings, adding: "And give glad tidings to the believers" (وَ بَشِّرِ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ).

In a Hadith, it is narrated that during the "Night of Aqaba" (the night the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله met secretly with a group of people from Medina near Mecca and took their oath of allegiance), when the covenant was being made between the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله and the Muslims of Medina, Abdullah bin Rawahah said: "Make whatever conditions you wish in this covenant for your Lord and for yourself."

The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: "As for my Lord, the condition is that you do not associate anything with Him. As for myself, it is that you defend me just as you defend yourselves and your property."

Abdullah asked: "What shall we receive in return for that?"

The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله replied: "Paradise!"

[1]: "Ukhra" (another) is an adjective for an omitted noun such as "blessing" or "trait." Some have said the noun is "trade," but that seems unlikely.

Abdullah said: "What a profitable sale! We shall not turn back from this deal, nor would we accept it if we were asked to turn back!" (We will neither rescind it nor accept its rescission) [1].

Key Points

1- What is the "Near Victory" (فَتْحٌ قَرِيبٌ)?

The promised victory in these verses was repeatedly granted to the Muslims, both in logical arguments and on the battlefields. Regarding what is meant by "near victory," commentators have offered several possibilities. Many have interpreted it as the "Conquest of Mecca," some as the "Conquest of the lands of Persia and Rome," and others as all the Islamic conquests that followed shortly after the faith and Jihad of the Muslims.

Since the addressees are not only the companions of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله but all believers throughout history, the phrase "help from Allah and a near victory" (نَصْرٌ مِنَ اللَّهِ وَ فَتْحٌ قَرِيبٌ) has a broad and expansive meaning. It is a glad tiding to all of them, even though its clearest manifestation at the time of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the revelation of these verses was the Conquest of Mecca.

2- What are the "Pleasant Dwellings" (مَساكِنَ طَيِّبَةً)?

Among the blessings of Paradise, specific emphasis is placed here on the issue of clean and comfortable housing, specifically dwellings within the gardens of eternal Paradise. This is because...

[1]: Fi Zilal, Vol. 8, p. 87.

...one of the fundamental conditions for human comfort and peace is the matter of housing—specifically a dwelling that is pure and clean from any physical or spiritual pollution, where a person can rest with a tranquil mind.

Raghib says in Al-Mufradat: The meaning of "Tayyib" (pleasant/pure) originally refers to something that the external and internal senses enjoy. This is a comprehensive meaning that encompasses all the suitable conditions of a dwelling.

It is noteworthy that in the Quran, three things are described as sources of "Sakan" (tranquility/rest): the darkness of the night (وَ جَعَلَ اللَّيْلَ سَكَناً - Al-An'am: 96), residential houses (وَ اللَّهُ جَعَلَ لَكُمْ مِنْ بُيُوتِكُمْ سَكَناً - An-Nahl: 80), and harmonious spouses (وَ مِنْ آياتِهِ أَنْ خَلَقَ لَكُمْ مِنْ أَنْفُسِكُمْ أَزْواجاً لِتَسْكُنُوا إِلَيْها - Ar-Rum: 21).

3- The World is the Marketplace for the Friends of Allah

In Nahj al-Balagha, we read that Ali عليه السلام, in response to a pretentious person who was hypocritically condemning the world, said: "You are mistaken; the world is a great asset for those who are awake and aware." He then provided an explanation, including that the world is the "marketplace of the friends of Allah" (متجر اولياء اللَّه) [1]. Therefore, if in one place the world is likened to a "farm" for the hereafter, here it is likened to a marketplace where a person sells the capital he received from God back to Him at the highest price, receiving the greatest blessings in exchange for a meager commodity.

[1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Short Sayings, No. 131 (summarized).

Fundamentally, the use of the term "trade"—and a highly profitable one at that—is intended to stimulate the most important human motivations: the motivation to gain benefit and to repel harm. For this divine trade is not only profitable but also repels "painful punishment" (عَذابٍ أَلِيمٍ).

A similar meaning appears in verse 111 of Surah At-Tawbah: "Indeed, Allah has purchased from the believers their lives and their properties [in exchange] for that they will have Paradise" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ اشْتَرى‌ مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ أَنْفُسَهُمْ وَ أَمْوالَهُمْ بِأَنَّ لَهُمُ الْجَنَّةَ).

We have provided further explanation on this in the commentary of Surah At-Tawbah [1].

[1]: Vol. 8, p. 147 (under verse 111 of At-Tawbah).

Ayah

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ كُونُوٓا۟ أَنصَارَ ٱللَّهِ كَمَا قَالَ عِيسَى ٱبْنُ مَرْيَمَ لِلْحَوَارِيِّـۧنَ مَنْ أَنصَارِىٓ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ ۖ قَالَ ٱلْحَوَارِيُّونَ نَحْنُ أَنصَارُ ٱللَّهِ ۖ فَـَٔامَنَت طَّآئِفَةٌ مِّنۢ بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ وَكَفَرَت طَّآئِفَةٌ ۖ فَأَيَّدْنَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ عَلَىٰ عَدُوِّهِمْ فَأَصْبَحُوا۟ ظَـٰهِرِينَ (١٤)
O you who have faith! Be Allah’s helpers, just as Jesus son of Mary said to his disciples, ‘Who will be my helpers for Allah’s sake?’ The Disciples said, ‘We will be Allah’s helpers!’ So a group of the Children of Israel believed, and a group disbelieved. Then We strengthened the faithful against their enemies, and they came to prevail [over them]. (14)

Commentary

Be Like the Disciples

In this verse, which is the final verse of Surah al-Saff, the emphasis and stress are once again placed on the matter of "Jihad," which constitutes the main axis of the Surah. However, it pursues this issue through another path, presenting a matter even more significant than the title of Paradise and its heavenly blessings.

It says: "O you who have believed, be supporters of Allah" (يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا كُونُوا أَنْصَارَ اللَّهِ).

Yes, supporters of Allah—the God from whom all powers originate and to whom they return; the God whose power is infinite and invincible. It is remarkable that here He has called upon His servants to help Him, and this is an unparalleled honor. Although its meaning and concept refer to supporting the Prophet and His religion, a profound grace and mercy are hidden within this expression.

Then, it points to a historical example so they may know that this path has never been, and is not, without travelers. It adds: "As Jesus, the son of Mary, said to the disciples: 'Who are my supporters for Allah?'" (كَمَا قَالَ عِيسَى ابْنُ مَرْيَمَ لِلْحَوَارِيِّينَ مَنْ أَنْصَارِي إِلَى اللَّهِ).

And the "disciples," in response, said with the utmost pride: "We are the supporters of Allah" (قَالَ الْحَوَارِيُّونَ نَحْنُ أَنْصَارُ اللَّهِ).

And in this path, they rose to struggle against the enemies of truth: "And a faction of the Children of Israel believed (and joined the disciples) and a faction disbelieved" (فَآمَنَتْ طَائِفَةٌ مِنْ بَنِي إِسْرَائِيلَ وَكَفَرَتْ طَائِفَةٌ).

It was here that Our victory and aid rushed to their assistance: "So We supported those who believed against their enemy, and they became dominant" (فَأَيَّدْنَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا عَلَىٰ عَدُوِّهِمْ فَأَصْبَحُوا ظَاهِرِينَ).

You too are the disciples of Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله, and you are honored with the distinction of being the supporters of Allah. Just as the disciples prevailed over their enemies, you too shall be victorious, and dignity and honor in this world and the hereafter shall belong to you.

This subject was not exclusive to the companions and associates of the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله; rather, the followers of truth who stand in a continuous conflict against the people of falsehood are always among the supporters of Allah, and ultimately, victory will be their portion.

Veins of sweet water and veins of bitter water, Flow through the creation until the Trumpet is blown!

Key Points Who are the Disciples?

In the Holy Quran, the disciples of Jesus عليه السلام are mentioned five times, twice of which are in this Surah. This expression refers to the twelve special companions of Prophet Jesus عليه السلام whose names are mentioned in the current Gospels (Gospel of Matthew and Luke, Chapter 6).

As we have previously mentioned, this word is derived from the root "Haur" (حور), meaning to wash and whiten. Since they possessed pure hearts and serene souls, and strove in the cleansing of their own souls and spirits as well as those of others, this term was applied to them.

In some narrations, it is stated that Prophet Jesus عليه السلام sent each of them as his representative to various regions of the world. They were sincere, self-sacrificing, and striving individuals who loved Jesus عليه السلام intensely.

However, in Christian traditions, it is mentioned that one of them named "Judas Iscariot" eventually betrayed Jesus عليه السلام and was rejected.

Further explanations regarding this matter have been provided under verse 52 of Surah Al-Imran [1]. In a Hadith, it is mentioned that the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, when he met with a group from the people of Medina at Aqaba who had come to pledge allegiance, said: "Choose twelve people from among yourselves and introduce them to me to be representatives of their people, just as the disciples were to Jesus, the son of Mary" [2]. This also demonstrates the importance of the status of those noble individuals.

O Allah! Grant us the success to participate in this profitable trade that You have prepared for Your friends (Awliya), and to benefit from its great blessings. O Lord! The discord and fragmentation of the ranks of the Muslims of the world have made them falter before the enemies; grant such awareness and awakening that all the Muslims of the world may gather in a single rank like a "solid structure" (Bunyanun Marsus) against the bloodthirsty enemies. O God! Your religion will not remain without a helper or supporter; grant us the honor of being among those helpers and supporters.

Amen, O Lord of the Worlds. End of Surah al-Saff, 10th of Ramadan al-Mubarak, 1406 AH.

[^1]: Tafsir Nemouneh, Vol. 2, p. 426. [^2]: Al-Durr al-Manthur, Vol. 6, p. 214.
